sections.heading: Pearls
sections.content:
MCC can grow rapidly and metastasize early: 63% of primary lesions have grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis, and up to half of patients develop lymph node metastases [7,8-15].
Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is an essential staging tool: SLNB should be performed prior to or at the time of definitive excision, regardless of surgical approach. Rates of SLN positivity range from 30%-40% in clinically node-negative disease [19,46,49,94,127,180,181].
Imaging is encouraged for baseline staging: Occult metastatic disease resulting in upstaging has been detected in 12%-20% of patients presenting without suspicious H&P findings. Whole-body FDG-PET/CT is preferred at initial workup [151].
Immunosuppression is a critical adverse prognostic factor: MCC-specific survival is worse for immunosuppressed patients. Minimize immunosuppressive treatments as clinically feasible [19,38,40-47,114].
Checkpoint immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1 blockade) has transformed MCC treatment: Durable responses are improved with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade compared with cytotoxic therapy. Avelumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, and retifanlimab-dlwr are preferred systemic therapy options [MCC-D].
Adjuvant radiation therapy delay >7-8 weeks is associated with worse outcomes: RT should be initiated as soon as wound healing permits [240,241].
MCPyV oncoprotein antibodies and ctDNA are emerging surveillance biomarkers: Seronegative patients have higher recurrence risk; rising titers may indicate recurrence. ctDNA typically becomes positive prior to or at the time of clinical recurrence [55,121,122].
Ipilimumab + nivolumab combination is particularly indicated for disease refractory to PD-1 therapy, with an objective response rate of 31% in ICI-pretreated patients [22,291].
